refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support validation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report energy metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR isolates the system's functional responsibility (instrumentation and data provision) from the acceptance criterion (target satisfaction), which is a verification gate, not a functional requirement. This keeps the FR solution-neutral by not prescribing how energy efficiency is achieved (e.g., via motor efficiency, load optimization, route planning, or idle reduction), only that the system provides the visibility needed to assess it.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved, distance, cycles) over the agreed evaluation period, computes energy_per_ton, and reports it in a format accessible to the Site Manager and Safety Manager. Verify that reported values are traceable to sensor inputs and calculation logic.
sub_frs: Acquire and log energy consumption data from all major power sources during operation.
Acquire and log work output metrics (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, cycle count) during operation.
Compute energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work output) from logged data.
Provide visibility of energy efficiency metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Safety Manager) in near-real-time or post-session reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose energy consumption measurement into (a) instantaneous power acquisition from energy source, (b) continuous integration over evaluation period, and (c) independent work output quantification; correlate via post-processing to derive energy_per_ton ratio.

DP2: Embed energy metering at the primary energy source boundary (e.g., fuel tank, electrical supply point) with synchronized work-output counters (e.g., tonnage sensors on load path); compute ratio in real-time or batch mode.

DP3: Instrument the energy conversion chain (motor/engine efficiency, transmission losses, load application point) with distributed sensors; aggregate via a data fusion module that normalizes to energy_per_ton using calibrated conversion factors.

DP4: Establish a measurement architecture with three independent logical subsystems—(i) energy accounting (cumulative consumption), (ii) work quantification (cumulative output), (iii) ratio computation and reporting—each with defined interfaces and no direct coupling between energy and work measurement logic.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Establish a measurement architecture with three independent logical subsystems—(i) energy accounting (cumulative consumption), (ii) work quantification (cumulative output), (iii) ratio computation and reporting—each with defined interfaces and no direct coupling between energy and work measurement logic.

potential_sources: Risk: If energy and work subsystems share a common clock or synchronization mechanism without explicit interface contract, timing skew could invalidate ratio calculation.
Risk: If ratio computation logic is embedded within either energy or work subsystem rather than isolated, changes to one domain force changes to the other.

Risk: If acceptance criteria validation (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is hardcoded in the measurement subsystem rather than in a separate reporting/decision module, the measurement system becomes coupled to business logic.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves low coupling by enforcing separation of concerns: energy measurement is independent of work measurement; both feed into a stateless ratio computation module. This allows independent evolution of sensor technologies, calibration methods, and reporting formats. Synchronization is managed via timestamped data exchange, not shared state.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source boundary is well-defined and accessible for metering (e.g., fuel consumption, electrical meter tap). Risk: If energy is distributed across multiple sources or conversion stages, subsystem (i) must aggregate without loss of fidelity.

Assumption: Work output is directly quantifiable (e.g., tonnage on load path, distance traveled). Risk: If work definition is ambiguous or context-dependent (e.g., partial loads, idle periods), subsystem (ii) requires clear operational semantics and filtering logic.

Assumption: Evaluation period is contiguous and well-defined. Risk: If measurement spans multiple operational shifts or environmental conditions, ratio computation must account for temporal stratification or provide conditional reporting.

Assumption: Acceptance criteria E_target is static and known at design time. Risk: If E_target is dynamic or customer-negotiated post-deployment, reporting module must support parameterized thresholds without re-engineering measurement subsystems.

Assumption: Measurement accuracy and resolution are sufficient to detect compliance/non-compliance. Risk: Sensor noise, calibration drift, or quantization error could mask or falsely trigger acceptance criteria; requires explicit uncertainty quantification and validation protocol.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP4 to explicitly define: (1) Interface contract between energy and work subsystems (data schema, timestamp precision, update frequency); (2) Ratio computation algorithm and rounding/precision rules to ensure reproducibility; (3) Acceptance criteria evaluation as a separate reporting module that consumes the ratio and compares against E_target without modifying measurement logic; (4) Calibration and uncertainty management as a cross-cutting concern with documented traceability to customer-defined targets. This ensures the FR is satisfied with minimal coupling and maximum auditability for compliance validation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement architecture encompassing energy accounting subsystem, work quantification subsystem, ratio computation engine, and inter-subsystem interfaces. Owns all three logical measurement domains and their coupling logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports computed energy consumption, work output, and efficiency ratio metrics to external monitoring and logging systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives work commands and operational parameters that define work quantification baseline and measurement scope

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits measurement data and receives synchronization signals for distributed energy/work sensor inputs if applicable

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of measurement architecture, energy accounting, and efficiency reporting. All three subsystems (energy, work, ratio) logically belong to a unified NGHE measurement domain. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry export and Tasking input cross boundaries; internal coupling between subsystems remains encapsulated.

Poor containment: Vehicle is a platform/host, not a measurement logic owner. Energy accounting and work quantification would diffuse across Vehicle subsystems (propulsion, actuation, sensors). Ratio computation would require Vehicle to own cross-domain logic, violating separation of concerns. High coupling risk.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a measurement domain. Placing measurement architecture here creates severe diffusion: energy and work data would leak into command logic, and ratio computation would be misplaced in a control module. Violates single responsibility principle.

Very poor containment: Safety Services owns safety constraints and alerts, not measurement logic. Energy/work accounting and ratio computation are orthogonal to safety. Placing them here creates false coupling and makes safety module responsible for non-safety concerns. High diffusion and low cohesion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP4 (three-subsystem measurement architecture with defined interfaces and decoupled energy/work logic) is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) NGHE is the functional domain owner responsible for energy-to-work conversion and efficiency metrics; (2) all three subsystems (energy accounting, work quantification, ratio computation) are logically cohesive within NGHE's measurement responsibility; (3) the decoupling requirement between energy and work measurement is an internal architectural concern that NGHE can enforce through interface contracts; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link) naturally connect NGHE to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and monitoring systems without requiring measurement logic to diffuse into those modules.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle platform provides raw sensor data (energy, work) without abstraction, NGHE may become tightly coupled to Vehicle's sensor interface specifics. Mitigation: Define a sensor abstraction layer within NGHE that decouples from Vehicle's physical sensor implementation.

Risk: If Tasking commands include real-time work adjustments, NGHE's ratio computation may need to respond synchronously, creating latency coupling. Mitigation: Establish asynchronous measurement update cycles independent of Tasking command frequency.

Risk: If Safety Services requires energy/work thresholds for safety decisions, measurement logic may diffuse into Safety Services. Mitigation: NGHE exports threshold-agnostic metrics; Safety Services consumes them via SafetyAlert interface without owning measurement logic.

Risk: Distributed sensor inputs across Vehicle may require NGHE to coordinate multi-source energy/work aggregation, increasing complexity. Mitigation: Establish a clear sensor data contract and aggregation protocol within NGHE's energy accounting subsystem.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly addresses FR by establishing a three-subsystem measurement architecture (energy accounting, work quantification, ratio computation) with defined interfaces and decoupled logic. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe implementation technology), logically sound (separation of concerns), and verifiable against the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). The decoupling requirement between energy and work measurement is a sound design principle that reduces coupling risk and supports independent validation of each subsystem.

diffusion_risks: RISK_SENSOR_COUPLING: If Vehicle platform provides raw sensor data without abstraction, NGHE may become tightly coupled to Vehicle's physical sensor interface specifics, violating the decoupling intent of DP4. Mitigation: Define a sensor abstraction layer (e.g., EnergyDataPort, WorkDataPort) within NGHE that decouples from Vehicle's sensor implementation details.

RISK_TASKING_LATENCY: If Tasking commands include real-time work adjustments, NGHE's ratio computation may need to respond synchronously, creating latency coupling between command frequency and measurement cycles. This could compromise measurement accuracy. Mitigation: Establish asynchronous measurement update cycles independent of Tasking command frequency; buffer work adjustments and apply them at measurement epoch boundaries.

RISK_SAFETY_THRESHOLD_DIFFUSION: If Safety Services requires energy/work thresholds for safety decisions (e.g., energy budget exhaustion triggers safe shutdown), measurement logic may diffuse into Safety Services or create bidirectional coupling. Mitigation: NGHE exports threshold-agnostic metrics (energy_consumed, work_output, efficiency_ratio); Safety Services consumes them via a read-only SafetyAlert interface without owning measurement logic.

RISK_DISTRIBUTED_AGGREGATION: Distributed sensor inputs across Vehicle (multiple energy sources, multiple work actuators) may require NGHE to coordinate multi-source aggregation, increasing complexity and potential for data synchronization errors. Mitigation: Establish a clear sensor data contract (timestamp, unit, source ID) and aggregation protocol within NGHE's energy accounting subsystem; define aggregation semantics (sum, weighted average, etc.) explicitly.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. The three measurement subsystems (energy accounting, work quantification, ratio computation) are logically cohesive and belong within NGHE's functional domain. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal, crossing only at natural architectural boundaries. Alternative ownership candidates (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) exhibit poor containment (0.15–0.35) and high diffusion (0.65–0.85), violating separation of concerns. NGHE is the correct owner. Residual diffusion risks are manageable through interface abstraction and asynchronous decoupling.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from NGHE's ratio computation subsystem. Energy accounting and work quantification subsystems can be independently validated against ground truth (e.g., external power meters, manual work tallies). Measurement architecture with defined interfaces enables unit testing of each subsystem and integration testing of coupling logic. Telemetry export provides audit trail for post-evaluation analysis.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. NGHE Solution owns all three measurement subsystems and their internal coupling logic. Tasking provides work command baseline; Vehicle provides sensor data via abstraction layer; Telemetry exports results; Safety Services consumes metrics without owning measurement logic. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous. Interfaces are directional and minimal. No measurement responsibility is left unallocated or ambiguously shared.

recommended_revisions: REVISION_1_SENSOR_ABSTRACTION: Explicitly define a sensor abstraction layer within NGHE (e.g., EnergyDataPort, WorkDataPort interfaces) that decouples energy/work measurement logic from Vehicle's physical sensor implementation. Specify data contract (units, timestamps, source IDs, aggregation semantics) to prevent tight coupling.

REVISION_2_MEASUREMENT_EPOCH: Define measurement epoch boundaries (e.g., 1-second, 10-second cycles) independent of Tasking command frequency. Specify how work adjustments mid-epoch are handled (buffered, interpolated, or applied at next epoch). This ensures asynchronous decoupling and measurement consistency.

REVISION_3_SAFETY_INTERFACE: Establish a read-only SafetyAlert interface through which Safety Services consumes energy/work metrics without owning measurement logic. Define which metrics (energy_consumed, work_output, efficiency_ratio) are exported and which are internal to NGHE. Prevent bidirectional coupling.

REVISION_4_AGGREGATION_PROTOCOL: Document the aggregation protocol for distributed energy/work sensors (e.g., sum all energy sources, sum all work outputs, compute ratio). Specify handling of missing or delayed sensor data (e.g., timeout, interpolation, fallback). Include timestamp synchronization requirements.

REVISION_5_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERION_MAPPING: Explicitly map the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) to NGHE's ratio computation output. Define evaluation period boundaries, rounding/precision rules, and how partial-period measurements are handled. Ensure Telemetry export includes sufficient metadata for post-evaluation audit.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the NGHE Solution demonstrates strong logical coherence (0.92 containment, clear ownership, verifiable acceptance criterion). However, the gate results surface five concrete diffusion risks and architectural vulnerabilities that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Given the high safety criticality of this system, proceeding to lower levels without addressing these risks would propagate uncertainty into detailed design and implementation. The recommended revisions are not cosmetic; they establish critical interface contracts and decoupling mechanisms that directly affect measurement integrity, safety coupling, and verification feasibility. Revising DP and IM now to incorporate these mitigations will yield a more robust foundation for sub-FR derivation and reduce rework downstream.

revise.why: The DP (three-subsystem measurement architecture) is logically sound but lacks explicit architectural detail on sensor abstraction, measurement epoch boundaries, safety interface semantics, and aggregation protocol. The IM (NGHE module ownership) is correct but requires refinement to specify internal sub-module boundaries (EnergyDataPort, WorkDataPort, SafetyAlert) and their data contracts. Revisions should: (1) Elevate sensor abstraction from a mitigation note to a formal DP sub-component (e.g., 'DP4.1: Sensor Abstraction Layer'); (2) Formalize measurement epoch and asynchronous decoupling as explicit DP design decisions; (3) Define SafetyAlert as a read-only export interface in IM; (4) Document aggregation protocol and data synchronization semantics in DP. These revisions transform mitigations into design requirements, reducing diffusion risk from 0.08 to <0.05 and improving verification feasibility from HIGH to VERY_HIGH.

assumptions: Vehicle platform can provide sensor data via an abstraction layer without requiring NGHE to know physical sensor implementation details (e.g., CAN bus, analog, digital).
Tasking system can tolerate asynchronous work command application at measurement epoch boundaries without requiring synchronous feedback.
Safety Services can consume energy/work metrics as read-only exports without needing to own or modify measurement logic.

Distributed sensor inputs (multiple energy sources, multiple work actuators) can be aggregated using a deterministic protocol (sum, weighted average, or other) without requiring real-time synchronization across all sources.
Measurement epoch boundaries (e.g., 1-second, 10-second cycles) can be defined independently of Tasking command frequency without creating unacceptable latency or measurement gaps.
Telemetry system can export sufficient metadata (timestamps, source IDs, aggregation semantics) to enable post-evaluation audit and verification of acceptance criterion.
open_risks: RISK_SENSOR_COUPLING: Vehicle platform may not provide sensor abstraction, forcing NGHE to couple directly to physical sensor interfaces. Mitigation status: PENDING_REVISION. Requires explicit DP sub-component defining EnergyDataPort and WorkDataPort contracts.

RISK_TASKING_LATENCY: Tasking system may require synchronous work adjustments, creating latency coupling with measurement cycles. Mitigation status: PENDING_REVISION. Requires explicit DP decision on measurement epoch boundaries and asynchronous buffering.

RISK_SAFETY_THRESHOLD_DIFFUSION: Safety Services may attempt to own measurement logic or create bidirectional coupling through threshold-based alerts. Mitigation status: PENDING_REVISION. Requires explicit IM definition of SafetyAlert as read-only export interface.

RISK_DISTRIBUTED_AGGREGATION: Multi-source sensor aggregation may introduce data synchronization errors or missing-data handling ambiguity. Mitigation status: PENDING_REVISION. Requires explicit DP aggregation protocol and timeout/fallback semantics.

RISK_VERIFICATION_PRECISION: Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) may be sensitive to rounding, precision, and evaluation period boundaries. Mitigation status: PENDING_REVISION. Requires explicit mapping in DP and IM to Telemetry export format and post-evaluation audit procedure.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of EnergyDataPort and WorkDataPort interfaces (data types, units, timestamp format, source ID scheme, aggregation semantics).
Definition of measurement epoch boundaries (duration, alignment, handling of mid-epoch work adjustments, buffering strategy).
Specification of SafetyAlert interface (which metrics exported, read-only contract, no reverse coupling, threshold-agnostic metric definitions).
Aggregation protocol document (sum vs. weighted average, handling of missing/delayed sensor data, timeout thresholds, fallback behavior, timestamp synchronization requirements).
Mapping of acceptance criterion to NGHE's ratio computation output (evaluation period boundaries, rounding/precision rules, partial-period handling, Telemetry export metadata requirements).
Interface control document (ICD) between NGHE and Vehicle, Tasking, Safety Services, and Telemetry, specifying data contracts and ownership boundaries.

Safety analysis (FMEA or similar) confirming that sensor abstraction, asynchronous decoupling, and read-only safety interface do not introduce new failure modes or compromise safety-critical measurement integrity.
